name: gopls
description: Official Go language server providing code completion, go-to-definition, find references, rename, diagnostics, and formatting for Go projects
when_to_use: Always active for Go projects — provides IDE intelligence, real-time error checking, and code navigation

name: go build
description: Compile a Go project and its dependencies into an executable binary
when_to_use: To compile the project, check for compilation errors, or produce a binary for deployment

install.command: https://go.dev/dl/ or package manager (brew install go, apt install golang)

name: go test
description: Run unit tests, benchmarks, and examples in a Go project with built-in coverage support
when_to_use: After code changes, before commits, in CI pipelines — also use for benchmarking and coverage reporting
what_it_finds: Test failures, assertion errors, panics, race conditions (with -race), coverage gaps

name: go vet
description: Built-in static analysis tool that reports suspicious constructs such as Printf format string mismatches, unreachable code, and incorrect struct tags
when_to_use: Before commits and in CI — catches bugs that compile but are likely incorrect
what_it_finds: Printf argument mismatches, unreachable code, incorrect struct tags, suspicious assignments, atomic operation misuse, nil dereferences in tests

name: go generate
description: Run code generation commands specified by //go:generate directives in Go source files — used for generating mocks, protobuf stubs, enums, and other boilerplate
when_to_use: After modifying interfaces or protobuf definitions, before building when generated code is stale

name: gofmt
description: Official Go code formatter — enforces the canonical Go style with no configuration needed, ensuring all Go code looks the same
when_to_use: Before every commit to ensure consistent code style — Go community standard, non-negotiable formatting

name: golangci-lint
description: Meta-linter aggregator that runs 50+ Go linters in parallel with caching — the de facto standard linting tool for Go projects, replacing individual linter invocations
when_to_use: Before commits and in CI — single command replaces running dozens of individual linters, highly configurable via .golangci.yml
what_it_finds: Style violations, bugs, performance issues, security problems, unused code, complexity metrics, error handling mistakes, and more depending on enabled linters

name: staticcheck
description: Advanced static analysis tool for Go — finds bugs, performance issues, and simplifications with high signal-to-noise ratio and zero false positives as a design goal
when_to_use: In CI pipelines and before releases — more thorough than go vet, can also be used as a golangci-lint sub-linter
what_it_finds: Incorrect API usage, deprecated function calls, impossible conditions, unnecessary type conversions, missing error checks, performance anti-patterns

name: govulncheck
description: Official Go vulnerability scanner — checks Go modules and binaries against the Go vulnerability database, reporting only vulnerabilities that actually affect your code paths
when_to_use: Before releases, in CI, after dependency updates — focuses on reachable vulnerabilities to reduce noise
what_it_finds: CVEs in Go dependencies that are actually called by your code, with call stacks showing how vulnerable code is reached

name: gosec
description: Security-focused static analysis tool for Go — inspects source code for common security issues like SQL injection, hardcoded credentials, weak crypto, and command injection
when_to_use: In CI pipelines, security audits, and before releases — complements govulncheck by scanning your own code for vulnerabilities
what_it_finds: SQL injection, command injection, hardcoded credentials, weak cryptographic primitives, insecure TLS configurations, file permission issues, integer overflows

name: go mod tidy
description: Clean up go.mod and go.sum by adding missing dependencies and removing unused ones — ensures the module files accurately reflect the import graph
when_to_use: After adding or removing imports, before committing, when go.mod is out of sync with source code

name: delve
description: Full-featured debugger for Go — supports breakpoints, stepping, goroutine inspection, variable evaluation, and conditional breakpoints with both CLI and DAP modes
when_to_use: When debugging runtime issues, inspecting goroutine states, stepping through complex logic, or attaching to running processes
what_it_finds: Runtime bugs, goroutine deadlocks, incorrect variable states, logic errors

name: pprof
description: Built-in Go profiling tool for CPU, memory, goroutine, and block profiling — generates flame graphs, call graphs, and top function reports from profile data
when_to_use: When diagnosing performance bottlenecks, memory leaks, excessive allocations, or goroutine leaks in Go applications
what_it_finds: CPU hotspots, memory allocation patterns, goroutine leaks, blocking operations, mutex contention

name: goreleaser
description: Release automation tool for Go — builds cross-platform binaries, creates GitHub/GitLab releases, publishes Docker images, Homebrew taps, and Snapcraft packages from a single config
when_to_use: When publishing releases, creating distribution packages, automating the release pipeline in CI

name: air
description: Live-reload development tool for Go — watches for file changes and automatically rebuilds and restarts the application, significantly speeding up the development loop
when_to_use: During local development to auto-rebuild on save — replaces manual stop/build/run cycles

name: revive
description: Fast, configurable, extensible Go linter — drop-in replacement for golint with 30+ built-in rules and support for custom rules via TOML config
when_to_use: When you need a configurable linter with custom rules beyond what golint provided, or as a golangci-lint sub-linter
what_it_finds: Exported function naming, error return conventions, unexported type usage, package comments, receiver naming

name: goimports
description: Automatically manages Go import statements — adds missing imports, removes unused ones, and applies gofmt formatting in a single pass
when_to_use: Before every commit as a superset of gofmt — especially useful when adding new package references or cleaning up imports

name: gofumpt
description: Stricter gofmt — enforces additional formatting rules beyond the standard gofmt such as grouped imports, no empty lines at block starts, and simplified composite literals
when_to_use: When you want stricter formatting than gofmt — commonly used as the formatter in golangci-lint configs

name: ko
description: Build and deploy Go applications to Kubernetes without Dockerfiles — produces minimal container images directly from Go source using the Go toolchain
when_to_use: When deploying Go services to Kubernetes and you want fast, reproducible container builds without writing Dockerfiles

name: testify
description: Popular Go testing toolkit providing assertion functions, mock objects, and test suites — the most widely used third-party testing library in the Go ecosystem
when_to_use: When writing tests that benefit from readable assertions (assert/require), mock generation, or test suite organization with setup/teardown

name: ginkgo
description: BDD-style testing framework for Go — provides Describe/Context/It blocks, parallel test execution, and rich test output with Gomega matchers
when_to_use: For large test suites benefiting from BDD-style organization, parallel execution, and expressive matchers — popular in Kubernetes ecosystem

name: gomock
description: Official Go mocking framework — generates type-safe mock implementations of interfaces for use in unit tests with expectation-based verification
when_to_use: When unit testing code that depends on interfaces and you need precise control over mock behavior and call verification

name: godoc
description: Documentation server for Go packages — serves browsable HTML docs from source code comments, including examples and cross-referenced types
when_to_use: When browsing or reviewing Go package documentation locally, or generating docs for internal packages not on pkg.go.dev

name: swag
description: Automatically generate RESTful API documentation from Go source code annotations — produces Swagger/OpenAPI 2.0 specs from comment-based annotations
when_to_use: When building REST APIs in Go and you want auto-generated Swagger docs from code annotations — supports Gin, Echo, Fiber, and net/http

name: buf
description: Modern Protocol Buffers toolchain — linting, breaking change detection, code generation, and dependency management for protobuf schemas
when_to_use: When working with Protocol Buffers in Go projects — replaces raw protoc with a faster, more reliable workflow including lint and breaking change checks

name: protoc-gen-go
description: Official Go code generator for Protocol Buffers — produces Go structs, serialization methods, and gRPC client/server stubs from .proto files
when_to_use: When generating Go code from .proto definitions — required for any Go project using gRPC or Protocol Buffers

name: wire
description: Compile-time dependency injection for Go — generates initialization code from provider functions, eliminating reflection-based DI and ensuring type safety at build time
when_to_use: When building large Go applications that need structured dependency injection without runtime overhead or reflection

name: uber fx
description: Uber's dependency injection framework for Go — provides lifecycle management, modular application composition, and constructor-based injection for production services
when_to_use: When building large Go services that need runtime dependency injection with lifecycle hooks, modular initialization, and graceful shutdown

name: cobra
description: The most popular Go library for building CLI applications — provides command hierarchy, flag parsing, shell completion, and man page generation used by Kubernetes, Hugo, and GitHub CLI
when_to_use: When building any non-trivial CLI application in Go — provides subcommands, flags, help generation, and shell completions out of the box

name: viper
description: Complete configuration solution for Go applications — supports JSON, TOML, YAML, envfiles, environment variables, remote config, and live watching with a unified API
when_to_use: When your Go application needs multi-source configuration with env var overrides, config file watching, and structured binding — pairs naturally with Cobra for CLI apps

name: ent
description: Entity framework for Go by Meta — generates type-safe Go code from a schema graph including CRUD operations, edges, migrations, and GraphQL integration
when_to_use: When building Go applications with relational databases and you want a type-safe ORM with auto-generated queries, migrations, and graph traversals

name: sqlc
description: Generate type-safe Go code from SQL queries — you write SQL, sqlc generates fully typed Go functions with no runtime reflection, supporting PostgreSQL, MySQL, and SQLite
when_to_use: When you prefer writing raw SQL over an ORM and want compile-time type safety for queries, params, and results

name: errcheck
description: Detects unchecked errors in Go code — finds places where error return values are silently discarded, a common source of subtle bugs
when_to_use: In CI pipelines alongside go vet — catches the most common Go mistake of ignoring returned errors
what_it_finds: Unchecked error returns, ignored deferred close errors, discarded function results

name: goleak
description: Goroutine leak detector by Uber — verifies that no unexpected goroutines are left running after tests complete, preventing resource leaks in production
when_to_use: In test suites for long-running Go services to catch goroutine leaks that would accumulate in production
what_it_finds: Goroutine leaks from unclosed channels, forgotten context cancellations, background workers not properly shut down

name: mockery
description: Auto-generate testify-compatible mock implementations for Go interfaces — produces ready-to-use mock structs with expectation methods from interface definitions
when_to_use: When using testify mocks and you want automatic mock generation from interfaces rather than writing mocks by hand

name: golang-migrate
description: Database migration tool for Go — supports PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQLite, MongoDB, and 20+ other databases with up/down migrations in SQL or Go
when_to_use: When managing database schema changes in Go applications — supports both CLI usage and Go library embedding

name: gotestsum
description: Enhanced go test runner with human-readable output, JUnit XML reports, and CI-friendly formatting — wraps go test with better UX and reporting
when_to_use: In CI pipelines for structured test output, or locally for cleaner test result formatting than raw go test

name: Task
description: Task runner and build tool written in Go — simpler alternative to Make with YAML-based task definitions, cross-platform support, and built-in file watching
when_to_use: When you need a cross-platform task runner for Go projects that is simpler than Make and supports file-based change detection

name: golangci-lint-langserver
description: Language server protocol wrapper for golangci-lint — provides real-time linting diagnostics in editors that support LSP, complementing gopls
when_to_use: When you want golangci-lint results displayed as editor diagnostics alongside gopls analysis

name: goose
description: Database migration tool for Go — simple, standalone binary supporting SQL and Go migrations with versioned up/down steps for PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQLite, and more
when_to_use: When you want a lightweight, zero-dependency migration tool that can be embedded as a library or used as a standalone CLI
